△ TRUEFORM
Munich AI Hackathon · Kyrall track · backend brief v0.3
drawing → verified, editable CAD — what we built

A drawing in. A solid you can trust, and edit, out.

Trueform reads an orthographic engineering drawing and rebuilds it as a real parametric STEP model — then proves it is right three ways: an AI looks at it, an independent judge double-checks, and the geometry is measured against every callout. One Gemini conversation runs the whole loop.

InputDrawing imagemulti-view orthographic PNG OutputSTEP / STL modeleditable B-rep, dimension-checked EngineOne Gemini conversationanalyze · generate · verify · refine
input · orthographic drawing
RECONSTRUCTparametric · verified
output · editable solid (real run)

FIG.01 — real result: a multi-flange manifold, reconstructed and verified on the first attempt.

01 / the problem

A drawing is not a model.

Industry runs on millions of legacy 2D drawings with no 3D counterpart. To remake a part, an engineer rebuilds it by hand in CAD — slow, and exactly the structured visual reasoning a model should do. But two things make it hard.

It must be editable

The output has to be a parametric STEP B-rep, not a mesh. A triangle soup looks fine and is useless — you cannot change a wall thickness or re-fit a bore on it.

Looking right isn't enough

Inferring one solid consistent across three views is genuine spatial reasoning, and vision models are weak at it. A part that looks right but measures wrong is worthless. So checking is the product, not an afterthought.

02 / overview

One loop: understand → build → prove → fix.

Everything happens inside a single Gemini conversation, so each step carries the full context of the ones before it. The agent reasons about the drawing, writes CadQuery code (for complex parts, one feature at a time), runs it in a sandbox, then verifies the result with three independent signals — the eye, an independent judge, and the ruler — and refines until they agree.

one conversation · full context sandboxed code execution 3 verifiers · eye + judge + ruler staged build · complex parts keep-best · never ship a regression
03 / the pipeline, as built

Six steps, three Python packages.

The backend is real and runs end-to-end today. Each step maps to a module; the LLM layer, the sandboxed CAD runner, and the agent that orchestrates the loop.

Step 01 · read

Analyze

Gemini studies the drawing first — what the part is, a per-view breakdown, and a structured dimension table (every callout with value, tolerance and a typed kind). This table is also the ground truth the size check uses later.

backend/agent/analysis.py
Step 02 · build

Generate

Grounded by its own analysis, an embedded CadQuery manual (mental model, design steps, pitfalls), and a few CadQuery examples fetched live (Tavily), the model writes a parametric script — Z-up, real CAD operations, one solid. Complex parts are built feature-by-feature, locking each stage that renders.

backend/agent/prompts.py · retrieval.py
Step 03 · run

Execute & repair

The untrusted script runs in a sandboxed subprocess (scrubbed env, time-limited). If it errors, the traceback goes back into the same conversation and the model fixes it — cheaply, before any verification.

backend/cad/render.py · _harness.py
Step 04 · observe

Render & measure

From the solid we export STEP/STL and render four views (front/top/side/iso). We also measure the B-rep: bounding box, hole patterns (count, pitch, bolt-circle), and a connectivity count.

backend/cad/_harness.py
Step 05 · prove

Verify (3 signals)

Shape: Gemini compares the renders to the drawing. Judge: an independent Pro model double-checks any claimed pass. Size: a numeric gate checks the geometry against the dimension table. Verified only if all three agree.

backend/agent/agent.py · gate.py
Step 06 · fix

Refine (keep best)

If a check fails, the critique and failing numbers are fed back and the model edits the best working script so far — a minimal change, not a rewrite, to avoid breaking what works. Bounded iterations with elitism: we always return the best candidate, never a regression.

backend/agent/agent.py
04 / the idea that wins

We don’t just generate. We verify — three ways.

The eye, an independent second opinion, and the ruler catch different errors, so we use all three and divide the labour cleanly: a vision critique and a separate judge model judge shape; the geometry kernel judges size. None is asked to do another’s job.

Signal 1 · shape (the eye)

Does it look like the part?

In-conversation, Gemini compares the four rendered views to the drawing and flags only obvious, structural differences: a missing or extra feature, a bore that should be open but is solid, a major feature on the wrong face, or two parts that should be one but are split. Exact sizes go to the gate; small details are deliberately ignored, so a good model isn’t nitpicked into a worse one.

A deterministic connectivity check backs it up: more than one solid means the part fell apart — caught instantly, no LLM needed.

Signal 2 · second opinion (the judge)

Would a fresh reviewer agree?

The self-critique shares the generator’s context and tends to rationalize its own intent — a model can look right in one view yet be subtly wrong in another. So before we trust a pass, an independent judge weighs in: a stronger model (gemini-3.1-pro-preview) with no system prompt and no history, shown only the drawing and the renders.

It runs only to confirm a claimed pass, applies a high bar, and vetoes false positives the in-context critic waves through. A model is “verified” only when the eye, the judge, and the ruler all agree.

Signal 3 · size (the ruler)

Does it measure right?

We query the actual solid and check each callout within tolerance. Beyond overall size and bore diameters, the gate verifies hole patterns precisely — count, hole-to-hole pitch, and bolt-circle diameter — per flange, catching mis-placed screws the eye cannot judge. Every verdict reports its coverage (e.g. PASS · 12/12 verified), so a thin check never masquerades as a strong one.

Dimensionkindspecbuiltstatus
Circular flange bolt circlebolt_circle47.547.5PASS
Circular flange hole counthole_count44PASS
Square flange hole pitchhole_pitch4040PASS
Oval flange hole counthole_count22PASS
Main bore ⌀hole_diameter2020PASS
Branch distancespacing51.25—N/A

Real run — 12 of 12 measurable dims passed. N/A = a kind we don’t measure yet (honest, never a false pass).

The gate has teeth. Inject a fault — build a 30 mm bolt pattern where the drawing calls 40 — and the gate fails it deterministically: “hole pitch: expected 40 ±0.5 mm but measured 30 mm”. The eye would wave that through; the ruler does not.

05 / under the hood

Two mechanisms worth a closer look.

The independent judge is how we stop the model grading its own homework. The staged build is how we keep a complex part from collapsing under one giant script. Here is exactly how each works.

The independent judge — fresh eyes on every claimed pass

Large models are good at justifying their own output. The self-critique runs as a turn inside the generation conversation — it has seen the analysis, the code, and the model’s stated intent — so it is lenient on its own work (it once approved a part whose towers were visibly staggered). The fix is a reviewer that shares none of that context. The judge is a separate, stronger model called with no system prompt and no history; it is handed only the original drawing and the four renders and asked one question: is this a faithful reconstruction? It fires only when the cheap self-critique already claims a pass (≈ one extra call per run) and exists purely to catch false positives. Its bar is high — only obvious, structural errors — so it vetoes nonsense without nitpicking a good model.

no system prompt · no history only drawing + renders runs only on a claimed pass high bar · obvious errors only eye + judge + ruler must agree

The staged build — one verified feature at a time

A single 200-line script for a complex part is brittle: fix one feature and the model rewrites the whole thing, breaking three others (we watched a part oscillate for four iterations). The staged build avoids that. First it plans the part as an ordered list of features — every positive solid first, then every cut. Then it builds them one at a time: each stage is handed the last script that rendered correctly and asked to add exactly one feature. Every stage is rendered, its connectivity checked, and locked before the next is added — so a feature can only ever stack on a base that already works. The result is a structurally sound model the normal refine loop then polishes. It runs automatically for complex parts and falls back to the fast one-shot path for simple ones.

plan · solids then cuts extend the locked script (+1 feature) render + connectivity each stage auto for complex parts falls back to one-shot

Watch it build — 11 locked stages, one real run

A pivot bracket, reconstructed feature-by-feature. The drawing goes in; each stage adds exactly one feature to the last script that rendered, and is locked before the next. Every frame below is a real render straight from that run — nothing staged for the slide.

11 stagessolids → cuts
drawing in → verified solid out — constructed in 11 locked stages (stage 11, the final bore, shown at right)

Stages 01–10, each rendered, connectivity-checked, and locked before the next is added. Stage 11 (the through-bore) yields the finished solid above — which then enters the normal three-signal verify loop.

Different jobs. The judge makes verification honest (it stops a wrong part being called right); the staged build makes generation reliable (it stops a complex part falling apart while being built). Together they attack the two ways a run used to fail silently.

06 / real output

What the agent actually sees and ships.

These are the exact renders from one run on the manifold drawing above — the four views the shape critique compares to the drawing, produced from the editable STEP solid the agent exported.

Verified on the first attempt. No code repairs, no refine iterations — analyze → generate → render → both signals pass. Every run is saved to a timestamped folder with per-iteration artifacts and a full trace.md of the model’s reasoning and critiques.

07 / engineering & guards

How it stays robust

  • CTXOne conversation, many roles. Analyze, generate, repair, critique and refine are turns in the same chat — the model fixes its own prior code with full context.
  • JSONStructured output. Reasoning + code come back as typed JSON, so extraction is robust; salvaged gracefully if a reply is ever truncated.
  • SECSandboxed execution. Model-written CadQuery never runs in our process — only in a subprocess with a scrubbed environment and a hard timeout.
  • NETResilient calls. Transient API errors retry with backoff; long multi-step runs survive a dropped connection.
  • JDGIndependent judge. A separate Pro model with no context double-checks every claimed pass — the generator can’t rubber-stamp its own work, and the bar is high so good models aren’t over-corrected.
  • STGStaged build. Complex parts are assembled one verified feature at a time, so a new feature only ever stacks on a solid that already renders.
  • DOCManual + live examples. An embedded CadQuery guide (patterns + real pitfalls we hit) plus a few examples retrieved at generation time (Tavily) lift first-try quality.

Loop guards

repair code errors first anchored refine · minimal edit keep-best elitism connectivity must be 1 solid render survives a GPU hiccup Z-up · model centered on origin traces for every run

Stack

  • LLMGemini — one vision model for the loop, structured output, high media resolution; a Pro tier (3.1-pro-preview) as the independent judge.
  • CADCadQuery / OpenCASCADE — true B-rep, STEP/STL export, B-rep measurement.
  • RNDRVTK (headless) — clay-gray views with feature edges, so cuts read clearly to the critic.
  • RAGTavily — partner tech: live CadQuery-example retrieval, best-effort and optional.
  • ENVPython 3.12 · conda — lean, pinned, single base provider.
08 / scope & next
Built today (backend)

The full agent loop: analyze, generate (with live CadQuery-example retrieval), staged feature-by-feature build for complex parts, sandboxed run + repair, multi-view render + B-rep measurement, three-signal verification (shape critique + independent judge + dimension gate with per-feature holes), anchored keep-best refine, versioned traces.

Next

A web UI/API wrapping the loop (live drawing → spec → 3D viewer → pass/fail table); measuring more dimension kinds (thickness, feature spacing) to widen the gate’s coverage — the highest-value lever left; more hero part classes.

Honest scope. We win by nailing a few real part classes end-to-end with a passing dimension table — not by gesturing at every possible part. Today it’s a backend that demonstrably reconstructs and verifies a multi-flange manifold; the UI is the wrapper, not the substance.

TRUEFORM · drawing → verified editable solid Kyrall track · Munich AI Hackathon backend brief · v0.3
